aids.title: Provider vs Signer
aids.content:
**P**rovider = **P**eek (read-only, looking at data)
**S**igner = **S**end (can send transactions)

"**P**eek with **P**rovider, **S**end with **S**igner"

aids.title: ethers.js Key Classes
aids.content:
**P**rovider - Connect to network
**W**allet - Store keys and sign
**C**ontract - Smart contract interface
**U**tils - Helper functions

"**P**eople **W**ant **C**lear **U**nderstanding"

aids.title: The Library Analogy
aids.content:
Think of Ethereum interaction like visiting a **library**:

- **Provider** = Library card (lets you read books)
- **Signer** = Librarian authorization (lets you borrow/modify)
- **Contract** = A specific book you're working with
- **Events** = Notification when new books arrive

You can read with just a card, but need authorization to make changes!

aids.title: Event Listening Best Practices
aids.content:
**WebSocket** for real-time events
**HTTP** for historical queries
**Always remove** listeners when done
**Filter** by indexed params for efficiency
**Use** queryFilter for past events

aids.title: Transaction Flow
aids.content:
```
Build TX -> Sign with Signer -> Broadcast -> Wait
    |            |                |           |
  params      wallet          provider    receipt
```

Signer signs, Provider broadcasts!

aids.title: Quick Library Comparison
aids.content:
**ethers.js**: Small, TypeScript, modern, human ABI
**web3.js**: Larger, original, extensive API
**web3.py**: Python, backend/scripts, similar to web3.js

Recommendation: ethers.js for new projects
